verses.text: Now Naaman, captain of the host of the king of Syria, was a great man with his master, and honourable, because by him the LORD had given deliverance unto Syria: he was also a mighty man in valour, but he was a leper. Guidance: The chapter opens with one of the most striking character portraits in the Old Testament. Naaman is identified by five descriptors, each carefully weighted. (1) 'Captain of the host of the king of Syria' (Hebrew sar-tzeva melekh-aram) — commander-in-chief of the Aramean army; the highest military post under King Ben-hadad II. (2) 'A great man with his master' (Hebrew ish gadol lifnei adonav) — a great man before his lord; Naaman has the king's full confidence. (3) 'And honourable' (Hebrew u-nesu phanim) — literally 'lifted of face,' meaning highly esteemed in society. (4) 'Because by him the LORD had given deliverance unto Syria' — the chapter's most astonishing statement. The LORD had used the Aramean general to bring victory to Syria. Israel's God had been operating in Israel's enemy's victories. The chapter is making a theologically radical point. The LORD's sovereignty extends beyond Israel's borders; He directs the campaigns of pagan armies for His own purposes. The Arameans win their wars under the LORD's hand whether they know it or not. (5) 'A mighty man in valour' (Hebrew gibbor chayil) — same phrase used of David's mighty men; a warrior of personal courage. Then the devastating final word: 'But he was a leper.' Hebrew metzorah — a person with tzaraat (the Hebrew umbrella term for various scaly skin conditions; whether 'leprosy' in the modern Hansen's-disease sense or some other dermatological disease is debated). In Aramean society Naaman's condition was apparently socially tolerated — he was still commanding armies, still functioning at the court. Under Israelite law (Lev 13-14) tzaraat would have meant ritual exclusion from the camp. The chapter is showing us the universal human condition compressed into one man. Naaman has everything the world admires: status, royal favor, military success, courage. Yet he carries a body-disease that mocks all of it. The chapter is teaching us that achievement and affliction often coexist in the same person. The world's most successful are often the world's most secretly broken. The chapter is also setting up a profound reversal that will unfold over the next 27 verses: this pagan general, his disease unhealable by Aramean means, will encounter the LORD of Israel through the LORD's own captive — and his confession at the end will leave him more theologically clear than the kings of Israel themselves.

And the Syrians had gone out by companies, and had brought away captive out of the land of Israel a little maid; and she waited on Naaman's wife. And she said unto her mistress, Would God my lord were with the prophet that is in Samaria! for he would recover him of his leprosy. Guidance: The captive girl's witness. The chapter shifts to a domestic scene in Naaman's household. The Arameans had been conducting raiding parties into Israelite territory; on one such raid they had captured 'a little maid' (Hebrew na'arah qetannah — a small/young girl, perhaps a teenager) and brought her back to serve in Naaman's wife's household. She is unnamed; she is a slave; she is a child; she has every reason to bitter at her captors. Yet she speaks to her mistress with extraordinary spiritual generosity: 'Would God my lord were with the prophet that is in Samaria! for he would recover him of his leprosy.' Hebrew achalei adoni lifnei ha-navi asher be-shomron az ye'esoph oto mi-tzara'to — 'would that my lord [were] before the prophet who is in Samaria; then he would gather him from his leprosy.' Three remarkable features. (1) She has retained her faith despite captivity. Her family's covenant with the LORD did not survive only in the homeland; she carries it into exile. (2) She speaks well of her enslaver. She does not curse the man who profits from her captivity; she wishes him healing. (3) She knows about Elisha. The prophet's reputation has reached this child; she knows where he lives ('the prophet that is in Samaria') and what he can do (heal leprosy). Her witness is the wedge that opens the entire chapter. The chapter is showing us that the LORD often uses the powerless to redirect the powerful. The Aramean general's wife will hear from her slave girl about a prophet in Samaria; that information will pass to Naaman; that information will pass to Ben-hadad; that information will reach Jehoram; that information will deliver Naaman to Elisha. A long chain of consequential events begins with a captive child's two-sentence wish for her master's healing. The chapter is teaching us a foundational principle. The captive's testimony is no less authoritative than the king's edict. The LORD speaks through whom He chooses; the smallest voice can carry the largest revelation. The chapter is also showing us what faith in captivity looks like. The girl did not refuse to serve; she did not sabotage her mistress; she did not curse her enemies. She spoke a word of hope from her own theological framework. She bore witness from where she was placed.

And one went in, and told his lord, saying, Thus and thus said the maid that is of the land of Israel. And the king of Syria said, Go to, go, and I will send a letter unto the king of Israel. And he departed, and took with him ten talents of silver, and six thousand pieces of gold, and ten changes of raiment. And he brought the letter to the king of Israel, saying, Now when this letter is come unto thee, behold, I have sent Naaman my servant to thee, that thou mayest recover him of his leprosy. And it came to pass, when the king of Israel had read the letter, that he rent his clothes, and said, Am I God, to kill and to make alive, that this man doth send unto me to recover a man of his leprosy? wherefore consider, I pray you, see how he seeketh a quarrel against me. Guidance: Diplomatic miscommunication. The slave girl's words pass up the chain. The wife tells Naaman; Naaman tells Ben-hadad; the Aramean king dispatches Naaman with a state letter to Jehoram king of Israel. The state-gift accompanying the letter is enormous: '10 talents of silver, 6,000 pieces of gold, and 10 changes of raiment.' At standard ANE weights, 10 talents of silver is roughly 750 lbs / 340 kg of silver, and 6,000 shekels of gold is roughly 150 lbs / 70 kg of gold. The combined wealth would buy a moderate-sized estate. Plus 10 fine garments — luxury textiles worth significant sums in their own right. Ben-hadad has equipped his general to pay handsomely for a cure. The letter Ben-hadad sends Jehoram: 'When this letter is come unto thee, behold, I have sent Naaman my servant to thee, that thou mayest recover him of his leprosy.' Note what the letter does NOT say: it does not mention the prophet. Ben-hadad either misunderstood the slave girl's report or assumed (in standard ANE diplomatic protocol) that a king controls the religious specialists in his kingdom. He addresses the request to the king as if the king were the dispenser of cures. Jehoram's response is panic: 'He rent his clothes, and said, Am I God, to kill and to make alive...?' He tears his garments — the gesture of mourning or extreme distress. His complaint: 'See how he seeketh a quarrel against me.' Jehoram reads the request as a pretext for renewed war. Ben-hadad has sent an impossible request; when Jehoram fails to deliver, Ben-hadad will have a casus belli. The chapter is showing us the catastrophic miscommunication of court diplomacy. The slave girl's clear message — there is a prophet in Samaria who can heal — has been filtered through three royal courts and emerged as an impossible demand from king to king. The institutional channels of power have obscured the simple original word. Note also Jehoram's theological position. He correctly says he is not God; he cannot heal leprosy. But he does not say, 'Send Naaman to the prophet of the LORD.' He had Elisha in his own kingdom; the previous chapters have shown him aware of Elisha. Yet in this crisis he does not think of the prophet. The northern king's theological framework is impoverished. He knows the LORD's prophet exists; he does not naturally include him in his problem-solving. The chapter is teaching us that institutional power often crowds out spiritual resources. Jehoram thinks like a king, not like a covenant-king. Help is available in his kingdom; he does not know how to reach for it.

And it was so, when Elisha the man of God had heard that the king of Israel had rent his clothes, that he sent to the king, saying, Wherefore hast thou rent thy clothes? let him now come to me, and he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel. Guidance: Elisha intervenes. News of Jehoram's panic reaches Elisha. The prophet immediately steps in: 'Wherefore hast thou rent thy clothes? let him now come to me, and he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel.' The Hebrew yado ki yesh navi be-yisrael — 'he shall know that there is a prophet in Israel.' Elisha names the chapter's central agenda. This is not primarily about Naaman's leprosy; it is about Naaman knowing there is a prophet in Israel. The healing will be the means; the recognition of the LORD's prophet — and ultimately the LORD Himself — will be the end. The chapter is showing us prophetic ministry as theological education. Elisha is not just a healer; he is a teacher. The healing of one general becomes the curriculum by which a pagan court learns that the LORD has His representative in His land. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD often gives Himself to be known through specific dramatic encounters with the obscure outside. The healing of Naaman will be remembered for centuries (Jesus invokes it in Luke 4:27); it will function as testimony to Aramean and Israelite audiences alike. The chapter is also exposing the gap between king and prophet. Jehoram tore his clothes; Elisha sends a message: 'Send him to me.' The king did not think to send for the prophet; the prophet must send for the petitioner. The northern political structure has not learned how to integrate the LORD's representative into its problem-solving. Elisha must intervene to redirect what the king should have routed correctly from the start.

So Naaman came with his horses and with his chariot, and stood at the door of the house of Elisha. And Elisha sent a messenger unto him, saying, Go and wash in Jordan seven times, and thy flesh shall come again to thee, and thou shalt be clean. Guidance: The arrival and the prescription. Naaman arrives at Elisha's house — 'with his horses and with his chariot.' The Aramean general comes in full state-mode: military escort, royal chariot, the visible apparatus of an important man making a state visit. He stops at the door, presumably expecting Elisha to come out and receive him with ceremony commensurate with his rank. Elisha does not come out. 'Elisha sent a messenger unto him, saying, Go and wash in Jordan seven times, and thy flesh shall come again to thee, and thou shalt be clean.' The Hebrew halokh ve-rachatzta sheva pe'amim ba-yarden — 'go and wash seven times in the Jordan.' The prescription is striking on multiple levels. (1) Delivered by intermediary. Elisha does not even come out to greet the general; he sends a messenger. The Aramean commander, the highest military officer of a major regional power, is being treated by Elisha not even with the courtesy of a face-to-face encounter. The deliberate snub conveys: the LORD's prophet does not owe the world's powerful any deference. (2) The prescribed cure is humble. Wash seven times in the Jordan. No elaborate ritual; no expensive medicines; no impressive prophetic gestures; no recognition of the petitioner's rank. Just go dip in a river. (3) The river is specifically the Jordan. The Jordan was not Israel's most impressive river; the Damascus rivers Naaman will mention in v12 (Abana and Pharpar) were larger, faster, cleaner. The Jordan is the covenantal river — Israel's boundary, the river of Joshua's entrance, the river of Elijah's parting. The cure depends not on the river's quality but on its covenantal identity. (4) Seven times. The covenantal number — completion, totality. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's cures often come in forms designed to humble the petitioner before they heal. Naaman expected a dramatic ceremony befitting his rank; he gets a dismissive instruction to wash in a foreign river. The shape of the cure is itself part of the cure: it strips away pride before it heals the body.

And Elisha sent a messenger unto him, saying, Go and wash in Jordan seven times, and thy flesh shall come again to thee, and thou shalt be clean. Guidance: The arrival and the prescription. But Naaman was wroth, and went away, and said, Behold, I thought, He will surely come out to me, and stand, and call on the name of the LORD his God, and strike his hand over the place, and recover the leper. Are not Abana and Pharpar, rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel? may I not wash in them, and be clean? So he turned and went away in a rage. Guidance: Naaman's outrage. The Aramean general explodes. 'Naaman was wroth, and went away.' Hebrew vayyiq'tzoph na'aman vayyelekh — 'Naaman was furious and went off.' He turns his back on the prophet's house and starts to leave. His indignation is articulate. He had imagined a different scene. 'I thought, He will surely come out to me, and stand, and call on the name of the LORD his God, and strike his hand over the place, and recover the leper.' Notice what Naaman expected. (1) A personal appearance — the prophet would come out, not delegate. (2) A standing posture — the prophet would stand before him, the appropriate respect-position. (3) An invocation of the LORD's name — 'call on the name of the LORD his God.' Naaman expected a religious ceremony with explicit invocation. (4) A hand-gesture over the affected area — 'strike his hand over the place' — a healing gesture making contact (at least gesturally) with the disease. He had pre-scripted the entire encounter; he came expecting the dignified medical/religious theater appropriate to his rank. He got a dismissive go-wash-in-the-river. Then his geographic complaint: 'Are not Abana and Pharpar, rivers of Damascus, better than all the waters of Israel?' The Abana (modern Barada) and Pharpar are the two clear, cold mountain rivers of Damascus — fed by the snowmelt of Mount Hermon, famously clean. The Jordan, in contrast, was a slow muddy river, lower-elevation, less impressive. Naaman's complaint: if washing in a river is the prescription, why not in the better rivers? 'May I not wash in them, and be clean?' He turns and goes off in a rage. The chapter is showing us how pride resists humble cures. Naaman's protests are reasonable — the Damascus rivers really are physically better water; the prophet really has been dismissive. But all of his objections miss the actual point. The healing is not in the river's water but in the LORD's word. The Jordan is the place because the LORD has named it; the Damascus rivers, despite their superior water quality, are not the appointed place. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's cures often come in forms our reason can object to. We want cures that match our dignity; the LORD gives cures that subvert our dignity. Naaman is offered the gift; his pride is making him walk away from it.

Naaman is offered the gift; his pride is making him walk away from it.

And his servants came near, and spake unto him, and said, My father, if the prophet had bid thee do some great thing, wouldest thou not have done it? how much rather then, when he saith to thee, Wash, and be clean? Then went he down, and dipped himself seven times in Jordan, according to the saying of the man of God: and his flesh came again like unto the flesh of a little child, and he was clean. Guidance: The servants' wisdom and the healing. As in the captive girl's case, the chapter is bringing about the central revelation through subordinate voices. Naaman's own servants — perhaps officers in his entourage — approach respectfully and reason with him. 'My father, if the prophet had bid thee do some great thing, wouldest thou not have done it?' The address 'my father' (Hebrew avi) is the respectful term of subordinates for a beloved leader. Their argument is simple and devastating: if the prophet had commanded an elaborate or expensive cure, you would have complied without protest. Why object to a small command? 'How much rather then, when he saith to thee, Wash, and be clean?' The objection makes no sense if the goal is the cure. Naaman receives the rebuke. He goes down to the Jordan. He dips himself seven times — Hebrew vayyit'bol sheva pe'amim — the verb tabal means 'to dip/immerse,' the root from which the noun for ritual immersion (tevilah) derives. He immerses, as the prophet had commanded, the precise number of times. The result: 'His flesh came again like unto the flesh of a little child, and he was clean.' Hebrew vayyashav besaro ki-vesar na'ar qatan vayyithar — 'his flesh returned like the flesh of a small boy, and he was clean.' The healing is complete and dramatic. Not just the disease eliminated; the skin restored to childlike freshness. Decades of leprous skin damage erased. The chapter is teaching us multiple lessons. (1) The LORD's cures require the petitioner to step beyond their pride. Naaman had to obey a command he disliked; the obedience preceded the healing. (2) The healing was not just symptomatic improvement; it was full restoration. The LORD does not heal partially; He heals to the root. (3) Wise counsel saved Naaman from his own rage. The servants' wisdom — speak respectfully, reason from your master's own values — is the chapter's quiet model of how to redirect a leader from a destructive impulse. The chapter is showing us the moment of obedience as the moment of grace. Naaman went down a furious leper; he came up a healed child. The seven dips are the visible signs of the surrender of the proud man.

And he returned to the man of God, he and all his company, and came, and stood before him: and he said, Behold, now I know that there is no God in all the earth, but in Israel: now therefore, I pray thee, take a blessing of thy servant. But he said, As the LORD liveth, before whom I stand, I will not take any. And he urged him to take it; but he refused. Guidance: Naaman's confession and Elisha's refusal. Naaman returns to Elisha — this time on foot, before the prophet, with his entire company. His confession is one of the great theological declarations of the OT: 'Behold, now I know that there is no God in all the earth, but in Israel.' Hebrew hineh-na yadati ki ein elohim be-khol-ha-aretz ki im-be-yisrael — 'behold now I know that there is no God in all the earth except in Israel.' The Aramean general — commander of the army that has repeatedly fought Israel — has just declared monotheism. He has not just acknowledged the LORD's local existence; he has declared the LORD the only God in the whole earth. The chapter is showing us pagan theology displaced by experiential evidence. Naaman had presumably grown up under a polytheistic framework (Rimmon, Hadad, etc.). The healing has revealed to him a higher reality: only one God is real; only this God can do what was just done; therefore there is no other. The chapter is teaching us that authentic conversion happens through encounter with divine action. Naaman did not become monotheist through philosophical argument; he became monotheist because the LORD healed him through specific obedience to a specific prophet. Naaman then offers the state-gift: 'Take a blessing of thy servant.' The 10 talents of silver, 6,000 of gold, 10 garments — the entire treasure brought from Damascus, now offered to Elisha as honorarium. Elisha's refusal is emphatic: 'As the LORD liveth, before whom I stand, I will not take any.' Hebrew chai-YHWH asher amadti lifnav im-eqach — 'as the LORD lives before whom I stand, if I take...' The oath-formula im is a self-cursing refusal. Naaman urges him; Elisha continues to refuse. The chapter is showing us prophetic integrity. Elisha will not accept payment for the LORD's miracle. The healing was not for sale; the prophet will not allow the impression that it was. Taking the gift would suggest the LORD's grace is a service available for purchase; refusing the gift declares it freely given. The chapter is teaching us a foundational principle of authentic ministry. There are cases when the servant of the LORD must refuse what would otherwise be generous compensation. Some moments require the message 'this is not for sale' more than any other message. Naaman must leave knowing that the LORD's healing was a gift, not a transaction.

And Naaman said, Shall there not then, I pray thee, be given to thy servant two mules' burden of earth? for thy servant will henceforth offer neither burnt offering nor sacrifice unto other gods, but unto the LORD. In this thing the LORD pardon thy servant, that when my master goeth into the house of Rimmon to worship there, and he leaneth on my hand, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon: when I bow down myself in the house of Rimmon, the LORD pardon thy servant in this thing. And he said unto him, Go in peace. So he departed from him a little way. Guidance: Two mules' load of earth and the Rimmon question. Naaman, having had his gift refused, makes two requests. First, an unusual request: 'Shall there not then, I pray thee, be given to thy servant two mules' burden of earth?' Hebrew masa tzemed-peradim adamah — 'a load of a pair of mules of earth.' Two mules' worth of Israelite soil — enough to construct a small altar-platform back in Damascus. His theological reasoning: 'Thy servant will henceforth offer neither burnt offering nor sacrifice unto other gods, but unto the LORD.' He is committing to exclusive worship of the LORD; but he needs the LORD's covenant land — even just a small portable platform of it — on which to do so. (His theology is mixed; he assumes the LORD is tied to His land; but his exclusive devotion is genuine.) The chapter is showing us a new convert working out his theology imperfectly but with sincere commitment. Naaman does not yet understand that the LORD is everywhere; he understands that the LORD has chosen Israel and he wants a physical connection to that holy soil. Second, a pre-emptive pardon request. 'When my master goeth into the house of Rimmon to worship there, and he leaneth on my hand, and I bow myself in the house of Rimmon: when I bow down myself in the house of Rimmon, the LORD pardon thy servant.' Rimmon (Ramman, 'the thunderer') was the Aramean storm-god, identified with Hadad. The king of Aram worshipped Rimmon; Naaman, as his chief officer, attended him; the king leaned on his arm during temple ceremonies; the prostrations of court were required. Naaman knows: when he gets back to Damascus, he will be required to attend the king to Rimmon's temple and to bow. He asks pardon in advance. The chapter is showing us a convert wrestling with the realities of his political position. Naaman cannot simply abandon his role; the king depends on him; the king's worship is part of his job. Naaman's request is theologically careful — he is asking pardon, not permission. He knows the bowing is wrong; he is not pretending it is fine. Elisha's response is brief: 'Go in peace.' Hebrew lekh le-shalom — 'go to peace.' He does not explicitly grant the pardon; he does not explicitly refuse it. He blesses Naaman's departure. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's prophets sometimes leave hard questions in the hands of the new convert's conscience. Elisha does not provide a casuistic ruling. Naaman knows his own situation; he will navigate it. The chapter is also teaching us that genuine conversion does not always produce immediate complete external transformation. Naaman the monotheist will still serve a polytheist king. The internal transformation is real; the external accommodations are works in progress. The LORD's grace extends to such intermediate situations.

But Gehazi, the servant of Elisha the man of God, said, Behold, my master hath spared Naaman this Syrian, in not receiving at his hands that which he brought: but, as the LORD liveth, I will run after him, and take somewhat of him. So Gehazi followed after Naaman. And when Naaman saw him running after him, he lighted down from the chariot to meet him, and said, Is all well? And he said, All is well. My master hath sent me, saying, Behold, even now there be come to me from mount Ephraim two young men of the sons of the prophets: give them, I pray thee, a talent of silver, and two changes of garments. Guidance: Gehazi's plot and lie. The chapter's tone darkens abruptly. Gehazi — Elisha's servant, who has appeared in supporting roles throughout chapters 4-5 — watches Naaman leave and conceives a private scheme. 'My master hath spared Naaman this Syrian, in not receiving at his hands that which he brought.' Gehazi disapproves of Elisha's refusal. He thinks the prophet should have taken the gift. The pejorative 'this Syrian' (ha-arammi ha-zeh) reveals the ethnic contempt at the root of his thinking; he sees the foreign general as someone who owes Israel and should pay. 'As the LORD liveth, I will run after him, and take somewhat of him.' The oath-formula chai-YHWH is exactly Elisha's. Gehazi uses the LORD's own oath-name to commit himself to extracting a private gift. The blasphemy is striking. He runs after Naaman. The general, seeing Elisha's servant pursuing, immediately stops his chariot. Naaman's hospitality is exemplary — he steps down from the chariot, walks to meet Gehazi, asks: 'Is all well?' Hebrew ha-shalom — 'is it peace?' He assumes some emergency must have brought the prophet's servant chasing him. Gehazi's answer is a lie: 'All is well.' Hebrew shalom — peace. The same word Elisha had used in blessing. Gehazi continues with a fabricated request: 'My master hath sent me' — false on its face. 'Behold, even now there be come to me from mount Ephraim two young men of the sons of the prophets: give them, I pray thee, a talent of silver, and two changes of garments.' Gehazi fabricates a story: two young prophet-school members have just arrived from the Ephraim hills; they need provisions; please send a talent of silver and two garments for them. The lie is layered: (1) Elisha did not send Gehazi. (2) No two young prophets have just arrived. (3) The 'gift for the prophets' is intended for Gehazi's own private use. (4) The amount requested is the negotiating opener — substantial but not so much that Naaman would refuse. The chapter is showing us the betrayal of the prophet by his own servant. Gehazi has witnessed the entire Naaman encounter. He saw the slave girl's witness; he saw the healing; he saw Naaman's confession; he saw Elisha refuse the gift. Yet his takeaway is: I should grab some of that wealth. The chapter is teaching us the danger of religious proximity without religious heart. Gehazi has been beside the prophet through every miracle of these chapters; nothing has shaped him; his fundamental orientation remains acquisitive. Faith does not transfer by association.

And Naaman said, Be content, take two talents. And he urged him, and bound two talents of silver in two bags, with two changes of garments, and laid them upon two of his servants; and they bare them before him. And when he came to the tower, he took them from their hand, and bestowed them in the house: and he let the men go, and they departed. Guidance: Naaman's overgenerous response and Gehazi's concealment. Naaman, far from haggling, doubles the request: 'Be content, take two talents.' Hebrew ho'el qach kikkarayim — 'be willing, take two talents.' He insists Gehazi accept more than requested. He binds the silver in two bags, lays them on two of his servants along with the garments, and has the servants carry the gift toward Gehazi. Naaman's response is itself a testimony: this newly-converted pagan general gives generously, doubles the request unbidden, sends his own servants to carry the load. He is grateful; he is also displaying the proper response to a divine miracle — he wants to give back. Gehazi, the Israelite, would extract; Naaman, the Aramean, would lavishly thank. The contrast is sharp. They travel to 'the tower' (Hebrew ha-ofel — a hill or eminence, possibly a fortified mound on the way back to Samaria). At this point Gehazi takes the bags from Naaman's servants and dismisses them — they go on their way without coming all the way to Elisha's house. The dismissal of the servants is critical to Gehazi's deception. If they had continued to the prophet's compound, the truth would have been exposed instantly. Gehazi 'bestowed them in the house' — Hebrew vayyifqod ba-bayit — 'deposited [them] in the house.' He hides the silver and garments somewhere in the prophetic dwelling. The chapter is showing us deception requiring active concealment. Gehazi cannot simply tell the prophet's compound that he received gifts; he must hide them in some unknown location. The lie has metastasized into multiple acts: false story, dismissal of witnesses, secret stash. The chapter is teaching us the bondage of a single act of greed. One act of acquisition has required Gehazi to dismiss the servants, hide the goods, prepare a cover-story. Each lie demands another lie. The chapter is also exposing the depth of Gehazi's contrast with Naaman. The pagan general gives more than asked, freely, immediately. The prophet's servant takes and hides, dishonestly, secretly. The Israelite has the inferior soul.

And Naaman said, Be content, take two talents. And he urged him, and bound two talents of silver in two bags, with two changes of garments, and laid them upon two of his servants; and they bare them before him. And when he came to the tower, he took them from their hand, and bestowed them in the house: and he let the men go, and they departed. Guidance: Naaman's overgenerous response and Gehazi's concealment. Naaman, far from haggling, doubles the request: 'Be content, take two talents.' Hebrew ho'el qach kikkarayim — 'be willing, take two talents.' He insists Gehazi accept more than requested. He binds the silver in two bags, lays them on two of his servants along with the garments, and has the servants carry the gift toward Gehazi. Naaman's response is itself a testimony: this newly-converted pagan general gives generously, doubles the request unbidden, sends his own servants to carry the load. He is grateful; he is also displaying the proper response to a divine miracle — he wants to give back. Gehazi, the Israelite, would extract; Naaman, the Aramean, would lavishly thank. The contrast is sharp. They travel to 'the tower' (Hebrew ha-ofel — a hill or eminence, possibly a fortified mound on the way back to Samaria). At this point Gehazi takes the bags from Naaman's servants and dismisses them — they go on their way without coming all the way to Elisha's house. The dismissal of the servants is critical to Gehazi's deception. If they had continued to the prophet's compound, the truth would have been exposed instantly. Gehazi 'bestowed them in the house' — Hebrew vayyifqod ba-bayit — 'deposited [them] in the house.' He hides the silver and garments somewhere in the prophetic dwelling. The chapter is showing us deception requiring active concealment. Gehazi cannot simply tell the prophet's compound that he received gifts; he must hide them in some unknown location. The lie has metastasized into multiple acts: false story, dismissal of witnesses, secret stash. The chapter is teaching us the bondage of a single act of greed. One act of acquisition has required Gehazi to dismiss the servants, hide the goods, prepare a cover-story. Each lie demands another lie. The chapter is also exposing the depth of Gehazi's contrast with Naaman. The pagan general gives more than asked, freely, immediately. The prophet's servant takes and hides, dishonestly, secretly. The Israelite has the inferior soul.

But he went in, and stood before his master. And Elisha said unto him, Whence comest thou, Gehazi? And he said, Thy servant went no whither. Guidance: The lie continues. Gehazi enters and stands before Elisha as if nothing has happened. Elisha's question: 'Whence comest thou, Gehazi?' Hebrew me'ayin gechazi — 'from where, Gehazi?' The question is searching. Elisha knows Gehazi has been gone; he asks where he has been. (The question is also pastoral — Elisha is giving Gehazi a chance to confess.) Gehazi's answer: 'Thy servant went no whither.' Hebrew lo-halakh avdekha aneh va-anah — 'your servant did not go anywhere.' A flat lie to the prophet's face. He could have confessed; he chooses to lie. The chapter is showing us the moment of decision. Gehazi has hidden the goods; he could now have come to the prophet, told the truth, and faced whatever discipline followed. Instead he doubles down. Confronted with the opportunity to confess, he chooses concealment. The chapter is teaching us that lies repeated in moments of opportunity for truth-telling are not just continuations of earlier lies; they are fresh moral choices. Each retelling commits the liar further. Gehazi could have ended the deception with a simple admission; he chose to extend it with a direct denial. The chapter is also showing us the depth of self-deception. Gehazi must have known Elisha was the LORD's prophet who routinely received supernatural revelation. He had seen Elisha know things that should have been hidden (the woman from Shunem; the LORD's withholding information; etc.). Yet he lies to the prophet's face as if he could get away with it. Greed has clouded his judgment so badly that he believes he can deceive the man through whom the LORD speaks.

And he said unto him, Went not mine heart with thee, when the man turned again from his chariot to meet thee? Is it a time to receive money, and to receive garments, and oliveyards, and vineyards, and sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and maidservants? The leprosy therefore of Naaman shall cleave unto thee, and unto thy seed for ever. And he went out from his presence a leper as white as snow. Guidance: The exposure and the curse. Elisha's response is devastating. 'Went not mine heart with thee, when the man turned again from his chariot to meet thee?' Hebrew ha-lo libbi halakh ka'asher haphakh-ish me'al merkavto liqratekha — 'did not my heart go [along] when the man turned from his chariot to meet you?' Elisha had been with Gehazi by prophetic vision the whole time. The prophet had seen Naaman step down from the chariot; had seen the exchange; had seen the bags; had seen the hiding. The 'heart' (lev) here means the prophetic perception that goes where the body does not. Gehazi's deception was visible to Elisha from the beginning. Elisha's diagnostic question: 'Is it a time to receive money, and to receive garments, and oliveyards, and vineyards, and sheep, and oxen, and menservants, and maidservants?' Seven categories of wealth — silver, garments, olive groves, vineyards, sheep, oxen, slaves. Elisha names the full menu of acquisition Gehazi was apparently planning to leverage (Elisha sees not just what Gehazi has taken but what he was going to use it for). 'Is it a time' — the rebuke. This is precisely the moment when the LORD's grace is being demonstrated to a pagan general; for the prophet's servant to extract material gain from that moment perverts the entire encounter. The healing was to demonstrate the LORD's free grace; Gehazi has secretly attached a price tag. The verdict: 'The leprosy therefore of Naaman shall cleave unto thee, and unto thy seed for ever.' Hebrew tzaraat na'aman tidbaq-ba ube-zar'akha le-olam — 'the leprosy of Naaman will cling to you and to your seed forever.' The disease Naaman has just been freed from is now transferred to Gehazi and to his line. The exchange is fitting in the moral logic of the story: Gehazi tried to take Naaman's wealth; he gets Naaman's disease instead. 'He went out from his presence a leper as white as snow.' Hebrew metzora ka-shaleg — leprous as snow. The whitening of the skin is the visible mark of the worst forms of tzaraat (Lev 13:13). Gehazi is instantly diseased; he leaves the prophet's presence already disfigured. The chapter is teaching us multiple grave lessons. (1) The LORD's prophet has eyes that see what his servant thinks is hidden. Religious proximity without integrity is exposed by the LORD's penetrating sight. (2) Greed for the wages of the LORD's miracle is one of the most severe forms of religious sin. Gehazi was not stealing from a stranger; he was selling the LORD's free grace as a private commodity. The disease that comes upon him is the appropriate consequence. (3) The contrast is dramatic. The pagan general entered diseased and left clean and confessing the LORD. The prophet's Israelite servant entered clean and left diseased and lying. The chapter has reversed the ethnic-religious expectations of its audience. The chapter is also preparing the reader for Gehazi's later appearance in 2 Kgs 8:4-5 — still alive, still a leper, telling Ahab's successor about Elisha's deeds. The disease is permanent for him, as the verdict promised.
